does: This paper derives a principled mathematical framework for safety alignment in language models by reformulating it as density ratio matching. The authors prove that the likelihood ratio of an optimal safe policy admits a closed-form decomposition, leading to Bregman Safety Optimization (BSO), a family of single-stage loss functions parameterized by convex generators. Each BSO variant minimizes Bregman divergences between data and model density ratios, provably recovering the optimal safe policy. The method requires no auxiliary reward or cost models and introduces only a single hyperparameter beyond standard preference optimization. Experiments on safety alignment benchmarks demonstrate that BSO consistently improves the safety-helpfulness tradeoff while maintaining simplicity and computational efficiency compared to complex multi-stage pipelines.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in direct preference optimization (DPO) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), which have shown that complex pipelines with separate reward models can be simplified through direct policy optimization. The paper extends these insights by addressing a key limitation: how to incorporate safety constraints in a principled way rather than through ad-hoc modifications like margin terms or multi-stage procedures. It connects to the broader literature on density ratio estimation and Bregman divergences in machine learning, applying these tools to the specific problem of aligning language models. The research also engages with prior work on constrained reinforcement learning and safety-aware objective design, offering a unifying perspective that recovers existing methods as special cases while providing theoretical guarantees.
enables: By reducing safety alignment to a well-understood problem in density ratio matching, this work opens several directions for future research. The theoretical framework suggests that other convex generators could yield new BSO variants with different safety-helpfulness tradeoffs, enabling researchers to systematically explore the objective landscape. The principled approach may facilitate integration of multiple safety constraints or human values through compositions of Bregman divergences. Beyond language models, the density ratio matching perspective could apply to safety alignment in other domains like embodied AI or vision models. The simplicity of BSO with a single hyperparameter suggests it may be more practical to deploy at scale and easier to tune than existing methods. Future work could investigate how BSO interacts with other alignment techniques, explore its sample efficiency properties, and develop adaptive variants that adjust the safety-helpfulness tradeoff during training.
